content:
5 min read
8 hours ago

--

If you’ve ever tried to customize a modern SharePoint page by embedding your own interactive charts, custom elements, or raw JavaScript code, you’ve probably hit the same wall as I did a few days ago.
You click Edit on the Home Page of your SharePoint site, add the Embed web part, paste your HTML code with JS in it, and instead of your pretty widget you see the raw code. 😒
The Embed web part is great for inserting HTML code, but JS tends to get blocked. You can’t add any external JS files and can’t really run custom meaningful & interactive code.
And, honestly, finding a safe, working solution took me way longer than I’d like to admit. Even asking AI for help just dragged me down rabbit holes of inaccurate and deprecated info.
Thankfully, I did stumble upon…PnP Modern Script Editor. It’s an app built and maintained by Microsoft 365 Patterns and Practices (PnP) community.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

In this article, I’m going to break down the exact steps to package, configure, and deploy this web part so you can finally inject custom HTML/CSS/JS right into your modern SharePoint pages.
Before we delve into the process, we need to understand the basics:
1 .SPFx (SharePoint Framework)
This is Microsoft’s approved way to build custom apps, web parts, and extensions for SharePoint online. Instead of modifying Microsoft’s SharePoint code directly, you can create an SPFx solution that SharePoint loads for you.
This is useful for creating/adding:

• Custom web parts

• Custom banners

• Custom navigation menus

• Chat widgets

• Consistent branding of your company
Since, Modern Script Editor is built as a web part, it also follows the SharePoint Framework architecture.
2. *.sppkg Packages
Sharepoint needs a package to install the SPFx solution and it is .sppkg. Think of it like .msi for Windows apps. It contains the web part, references to JavaScript bundles that make up the SPFx, permissions and deployment settings, and lastly asset metadata.
3. gulp
A command-line task runner for Node.js. In SPFx development, Gulp commands automate the heavy lifting — like compiling TypeScript, minifying code, bundling assets, and packaging the final .sppkg file.
To put in simple words, it just takes the source code and prepares it for deployment on SharePoint.
Prerequisites
You need to have Git installed to clone the Github Repo.
git --versionSPFx development is strict about Node.js version compatibility. If you have wrong version installed, your build will fail:
node -vEnsure you are using the correct LTS (Long Term Support) version of Node.js required for the SPFx version of the PnP web part you are downloading. Please cross-reference here!
Clone and Install Dependencies
Go to the official PnP Github Repo
Clone the repo:
git clone https://github.com/pnp/sp-dev-fx-webparts.gitGo to specific path in your local machine:
cd sp-dev-fx-webparts\samples\react-script-editorNext, download all the required packages to build the solution:
npm installCrucial Tweaks‼️
Enable Tenant-Wide Deployment
By default, you have to install this web part individually on every single site collection where you want to use it. To make it available everywhere automatically:

• Open config/package-solution.json

• Change “skipFeatureDeployment” from false to true:
"skipFeatureDeployment": trueAllow on Modern Team Sites
By default, this web part is blocked on no-script sites (which includes standard Office 365 Group team sites; usually SharePoint sites that are automatically created when Microsoft Team, Microsoft 365 Group, Planner, Outlook Group are setup) because it allows arbitrary script execution. If you need it on these sites:

• Open src/webparts/scriptEditor/ScriptEditorWebPart.manifest.json

• Find “requiresCustomScript” and change it to false:
"requiresCustomScript": falseNote: Only change this if you trust your SharePoint site owners, as this bypasses a default security governance measure.
Build the Package
Now it’s time to let Gulp do its job — bundle up and package the PnP script editor project files. Run these commands one at a time:
gulp cleanRemoves the temporary build files generated (temp/, lib/, dist/) from previous builds so you can start with a fresh build.
And, don’t worry, it doesn’t delete the source code.
gulp bundle --shipTakes the source code files, compiles TypeScript into JavaScript, bundles multiple files together, and optimizes the code. ship flag = production- ready!
gulp package-solution --shipCreates the SharePoint .sppkg package. It takes the component manifests, web part definitions, production bundles, and metadata and packages them all into pnp-script-editor.sppkg.
Once finished, navigate to the sharepoint\solution folder inside your project directory. You’ll see: pnp-script-editor.sppkg.
Deploy to SharePoint Online

• Go to SharePoint Admin Center

• Navigate to More Features > Apps > Open App Catalog

• Upload your pnp-script-editor.sppkg file

• Ensure you select the option to make it available to all sites (if you enabled tenant-wide deployment in the earlier section)

• Click Deploy
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Adding the Web Part

• Navigate to the SharePoint site where you want to inject your code

• Click Edit in the top right corner

• Click the + icon to add a new web part and search for Modern Script Editor

• Once added to the page, click Edit Markup

• Paste in your code under Edit HTML Code! You can include raw <script> tags, <style> tags for CSS, and standard HTML.

• Republish the page and reload your tab
Press enter or click to view image in full size

You’ve made it this far! 🥳
But, sometimes, you paste in your code, republish the page, and your script won’t execute.
If you open your browser’s developer tools (Right Click > Inspect > Console), you might see the red Content Security Policy (CSP) or CORS errors.
Wait, what’s happening?
In web development, a Content Security Policy (CSP) is a security feature that protects against Cross-Site Scripting (XSS), clickjacking, and code injection attacks. It dictates exactly which external domains are allowed to load resources (like scripts or images) on your page.
As of March 1st 2026, Microsoft strictly enforced CSP on SharePoint Online.
Now, when your browser tries to run an external script through the Modern Script Editor, SharePoint checks that script’s URL against its CSP rules. If your domain isn’t explicitly whitelisted, the browser blocks it. By default, SharePoint pretty much only trusts its own .sppkg deployment packages.
How to fix it?
The good news? It’s pretty straightforward.

• Look at the Console errors to identify which URL/domain was blocked.

• Head over to SharePoint Admin Center

• Navigate to Policies > HTML Field Security

• In Trusted Script Sources, add the domains flagged in your console.
Once you add those domains, SharePoint updates its CSP header rules to include them. Refresh your page, and boom — your custom JS script finally came to life!
